cards.front: What problem is Routh-Hurwitz designed to solve?
cards.back: It tests stability of a denominator polynomial without solving explicitly for every root.

cards.front: Which polynomial is used in the Routh-Hurwitz test?
cards.back: The denominator polynomial P(s) of the transfer function.

cards.front: What simple coefficient condition is necessary for stability?
cards.back: All coefficients must have the same sign.

cards.front: Why is the same-sign coefficient condition not enough?
cards.back: Because a polynomial can still have unstable roots even when all coefficients are positive.

cards.front: What kind of test is Routh-Hurwitz logically?
Why is direct root-solving unattractive for high-order systems?
cards.back: Because it becomes algebraically messy and often impractical, especially with tunable parameters.

cards.front: Why does the numerator not drive the Routh-Hurwitz test?
cards.back: Because stability is determined by poles, and poles come from the denominator.

cards.front: What is the key conceptual shift in this lesson?
cards.back: Moving from explicit pole calculation to coefficient-based stability reasoning.

cards.front: What is the practical question behind Routh-Hurwitz?
cards.back: How can we tell whether a denominator has no right-half-plane roots without factorizing it?

cards.front: What does the counterexample with positive coefficients teach you?
cards.back: It teaches that quick coefficient-sign checks are only filters, not full proofs of stability.
